coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots with photo-induced enzyme activity and room-temperature phosphorescence and their application in drug analysis and anticounterfeiting

coredata.dc:description: The carbon quantum dots with enzyme-mimicking activity and phosphorescence have exhibited promising applications in biochemical detection and anticounterfeiting. Therefore, this paper reports the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CDs@heme) with fluorescence, phosphorescence and photo-induced oxidase properties through the microwave method (5 min) using N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine, phosphoric acid, and hemoglobin as the raw materials. Also, CDs@heme were complexed with tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) to prepared CDs@heme-TEOS with water-soluble phosphorescence. The best excitation and emission wavelengths for the fluorescence of the prepared CDs@heme are 370 and 446 nm, respectively, while those of the phosphorescence are 350 and 530 nm, respectively. A sensitive detection strategy for carmine has been established with detection limits of fluorescence and phosphorescence at 330 and 51 nM, respectively. Based on the inhibitory effect of tiopronin (TPN) on the photo-induced oxidase activity of CDs@heme, a colorimetric detection method for TPN has been established in the concentration range of 0.015 − 25 μM, with a detection limit of 9.8 nM, and the recovery rate for TPN enteric tablets is in the range of 99.85 % − 109.21 %. The change can be visually presented with a hydrogel as the medium. A triple information security system was developed based on the fluorescence/phosphorescence/mimic enzyme properties of CDs@heme. This research provides a new perspective and strategy for room-temperature phosphorescence and photosensitive multifunctional nanomaterials.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots with photo-induced enzyme activity and room-temperature phosphorescence and their application in drug analysis and anticounterfeiting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon quantum dots with enzyme-mimicking activity and phosphorescence have exhibited promising applications in biochemical detection and anticounterfeiting. Therefore, this paper reports the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CDs@heme) with fluorescence, phosphorescence and photo-induced oxidase properties through the microwave method (5 min) using N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine, phosphoric acid, and hemoglobin as the raw materials. Also, CDs@heme were complexed with tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) to prepared CDs@heme-TEOS with water-soluble phosphorescence. The best excitation and emission wavelengths for the fluorescence of the prepared CDs@heme are 370 and 446 nm, respectively, while those of the phosphorescence are 350 and 530 nm, respectively. A sensitive detection strategy for carmine has been established with detection limits of fluorescence and phosphorescence at 330 and 51 nM, respectively. Based on the inhibitory effect of tiopronin (TPN) on the photo-induced oxidase activity of CDs@heme, a colorimetric detection method for TPN has been established in the concentration range of 0.015 − 25 μM, with a detection limit of 9.8 nM, and the recovery rate for TPN enteric tablets is in the range of 99.85 % − 109.21 %. The change can be visually presented with a hydrogel as the medium. A triple information security system was developed based on the fluorescence/phosphorescence/mimic enzyme properties of CDs@heme. This research provides a new perspective and strategy for room-temperature phosphorescence and photosensitive multifunctional nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: As an ideal alternative to semiconductor quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have attracted sufficient interest of researchers [1]; moreover, the design and development of nanomaterials with phosphorescent and catalytic activities are the hotspots of the present research, especially their applications in the fields of biochemical sensing, bioimaging, information security and encryption, catalysis and biomedicine [2].

For the present, CDs with both non-substrate-assisted room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and enzyme-like activities were rarely reported in published literatures. RTP materials exhibit obvious merits such as long lifetime, naked-eye luminescence, large Stokes shift, good stability, and high signal-to-noise ratio [3]. The RTP property of nanomaterials is usually acquired through embedding nanomaterials into matrices, such as polyvinyl alcohol, polyacrylamide, urea, boric acid, zeolite, gelatin and silica [4,5]. This strategy allows the formation of hydrogen or covalent bonds between a nanomaterial and a substrate, inhibiting the vibrating dissipation between the triplet and singlet states [6]. Meanwhile, the protective structure formed during the crosslinking process protects the RTP property of nanomaterials from the impacts of water or air [7]. However, the selection of proper substrate is quite time consuming, and the preparation process is complicated, significantly limiting the development of RTP materials [8].

The development of detection platform based on the optical signals of RTP CDs is now the focus of current researches. Phosphorescent nanomaterials typically exhibit longer wavelength emission after removal of the excitation source, leading to a larger Stokes shift compared to single fluorescence, which avoids interference and self-absorption of the excitation light [9]. The longer phosphorescent lifetime allows the selection of proper time gate for detection to avoid interference from system and light scattering [10]. Compared with normal detections based on the fluorescence signal change of CDs, multi-parametric or multi-responsive room-temperature phosphorescent CDs can effectively improve the selectivity and accuracy of detection, especially for the detection of samples that can produce fluorescence [11]. Therefore, the dual-mode detection platform based on the fluorescence/phosphorescence output of RTP-CDs exhibits great advantages like the minimum measurement difference and high efficiency [12]. Carmine is an artificial colorant that can produce aromatic amines and may lead to nucleic acid mutations. Prolonged or large-amount intake of carmine can be extremely harmful to organisms [13]. Therefore, the development of a fast, sensitive, dual-signal (fluorescence/phosphorescence)-sensing method to carmine is of practical value.

The CDs with high phosphorescence quantum yields and long phosphorescence lifetime is the ideal candidate for developing highly efficient light-activated oxidase mimics (photosensitizers). The use of common photosensitizers, such as transition-metal compounds, and noble-metal nanoparticles are considerably limited by their high cost, photobleaching, and high biotoxicity [14]. Silicon-based materials are of low cost and exhibit high stability and good biocompatibility, attracting considerable interests from researchers. Light-activated oxidase mimics can activate dissolved oxygen to produce reactive oxygen species (e.g. 1O2,O2 •−) [15,16] under the irradiation of visible light, which is one of the mechanism for the mimic enzyme activity. Since RTP is induced by triplet excited state leaps, energy transfer easily occurs between O2 and the excited triplet-state (T1) nanomaterials, leading to the formation of 1O2. As energy transfer is realized through intermolecular collisions, RTP CDs photosensitizers with longer exciton lifetime (longer RTP lifetime) exhibit higher efficiency in collision with O2 and energy transfer, greatly enhancing the sensitivity of O2 and quantum yield (QY) of 1O2 [17,18]. At present, the reports on the CDs with both light-activated oxidase mimics properties and RTP property are rare, but they have attracted extensive attention from researchers.

The colorimetric method based on the photoactivated oxidase property of CDs exhibits obvious merits like simplicity, sensitivity, low cost, and the detection results can be directly observed by naked-eyes [19]. In H2O2-free environment, CDs can activate dissolved oxygen to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) and oxidize the substrate, leading to color change [15,20]. Tiopronin (N-(2-mercaptopropionyl)glycine; TPN) is a drug used in the treatment of heavy-metal poisoning [21], cystinuria [22], and liver disease [23]. In clinical treatment, the dosage of TPN is a critical problem. TPN, as a thiol compound, can inhibit the activity of nanozymes, leading to fast, simple, and sensitive detection [24]. In particular, the embedding of a nanozyme into a hydrogel, along with the smartphone-assisted quantification technology, exhibited promising outlook in on-site detection.

The development of multi-functional RTP CDs provide possibility for high-level anticounterfeit applications. Compared with fluorescent materials, phosphorescent anticounterfeiting materials exhibit longer lifetime and higher stability [25]. Enzymatic color change counterfeiting is easy to observe by the naked eye and is often irreversible [26]. However, the actual performance of a single anticounterfeiting output mode is deemed unsatisfactory, and the need for a multi-anticounterfeiting channel mode is increasing [27]. The development of triple-channel anticounterfeiting label (fluorescent, phosphorescent, and enzyme-induced color change) is different from traditional static anticounterfeiting labels with encrypted single optical information, and it is often difficult to be replicated because it can even encode the optical information in spatial or temporal scales [26,28], which provides a bright future for high security-level anticounterfeiting applications.

This paper reports the rapid synthesis of nonassisted blue-fluorescent, yellow-green phosphorescent CDs@heme through a microwave method and it were complexed with TEOS prepared a water-soluble CDs@heme (CDs@heme-TEOS). A series of characterization methods have been used to study the structural and optical properties of the product. The fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of CDs@heme are 6 ns and 126 ms, respectively, with fluorescent/phosphorescent QYs at 7.59 % and 5.51 %, respectively. A CDs@heme-TEOS aqueous solution embedded with tetraethyl orthosilicate can realize phosphorescence emission, and based on its excellent optical properties, the detection of carmine has been realized. CDs@heme has been endowed with photo-induced oxidase activity by inducing heme, which is based on h+, O2 •- and e−. The combination of enzyme activity and a hydrogel system realized the sensitive colorimetric detection to TPN, which has been proven to have practical application through spiking recovery experiments. Moreover, a triple-channel anticounterfeiting label (fluorescent, phosphorescent and enzyme-induced color change) has been established. This study is of great significance to the development of multi-channel, multi-functional sensing nanomaterial systems and their applications in detection and anticounterfeiting fields.

First, 0.1 mL of DAMO was dissolved in 5 mL of DI water in a 25 mL beaker, then 0.15 mL heme (1.5 mM) and 0.1 mL H3PO4 were added under stirring. The beaker was transferred and heated in an oven (750 W, med-high) for 5 min. After the reaction, the beaker was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The obtained solid was dissolved in 15 mL DI water for ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The crude aqueous solution was centrifuged (9500 rpm) for 10 min, and large or agglomerated particles were filtered and removed with a filter membrane (0.22 μm). The pH of the collected solution was adjusted to neutral and dialyzed (MWCO: 500 Da) for 24 h (water was changed every 6 h). Finally, a white powder was obtained by freeze-drying for 18 h.

For synthesizing CDs@heme-TEOS, 15 mL of 100 μg/mL CDs solution was prepared using the solid obtained via the procedure reported in Section 2.1 and adding 0.5 mL of TEOS and 0.5 mL of NH4OH to it. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 16 h, then the solution was passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane several times to collect a transparent solution.

To prepare the PVM hydrogel, 0.5 g of PVA was weighed and added into 10 mL of water, the solution was stirred thoroughly in an 80 °C water bath until fully dissolved. Then, 2.5 g of AM, 5 mg of MBA, and 75 mg of APS were sequentially added to the solution. The resulting solution was sonicated for 1 min in an ultrasonic cleaner to remove bubbles generated by stirring. A 96-well plate was used as the mold, and 80 μL of the gel solution was added to each well and irradiated with a 365 nm UV lamp (4 V, 52 W) at room temperature for 1 h to obtain the PVM hydrogel.

With quinine sulfate (54.6 %) as the reference, the fluorescence QY of CDs@heme was determined using comparative law. To avoid reabsorption, the absorbance values of the standard solution and CDs@heme are required to be less than 0.10. QY was calculated based on the following formula (1):
(1)
Q Y s = Q r
I s A r
I r A s
where A is the absorbance, and I is the peak area of the fluorescence spectrum. The subscripts r and s represent standard samples and test samples, respectively.

40 μL of CDs@heme, 100 μL of different concentrations TPN, 100 μL of 3 mM TMB, and 760 μL of PBS (pH = 4) were added in a colorimetric dish (1 cm × 1 cm). After fully mixing, the UV absorption was measured at 400–800 nm, and the characteristic absorption of TMB was at 652 nm with the concentration of TPN as the A652–TPN concentration working curve.

Using fluorescence/phosphorescence to detect carmine, the emission spectra at the optimal excitation wavelength of fluorescence/phosphorescence were determined by adding different concentrations of carmine, and a linear graph of fluorescence/phosphorescence intensity D-value and carmine concentration was drawn.

350 μL of CDs@heme, 250 μL of TMB (3 mM), and 250 μL of PBS (20 mM, pH = 3) were mixed to prepare anticounterfeiting solution. The CDs@heme was coated on the password area, and the password was observed under UV light, then the anticounterfeiting solution was sprayed on the password area, UV light was thereafter used to observe the changes in the ink, and the photo was captured using a smartphone.

The RGB channel intensities were extracted using the well-known software ImageJ2 × 2.1.4.7. The RGB values were converted to the desired Commission International d’Éclairage (CIE–L∗a∗b∗) values using a Color Space Converter plugin in the same software.

With TMB as the chromogenic substrate, the photo-induced oxidase activities of the synthesized products prepared by different raw materials have been explored under the irradiation of UV-light irradiation (365 nm). Experiments show that the non-metal-doping CDs with DAMO, H3PO4 and KI as the raw materials exhibit RTP and weak photo-induced oxidase activity and can catalyze the oxidation of substrate from colorless into blue without the presence of H2O2. The long-lifetime triplet state of RTP CDs has been found beneficial to the transfer of excited triplet-state energy to dispersed oxygen molecules, producing single linear-state oxygen and superoxide radicals. These strong oxidizing radicals trigger the oxidation of TMB and its obvious color change, endowing CDs with photo-induced oxidase activity.

To promote the photo-induced oxidase activity of CDs, some common raw materials for nanozymes, such as CoCl2, MnCl2, FeCl2, GdCl3, HB and heme, have been used to investigate their effects on the catalytic activity of the products (Fig. 1b). The introductions of Mn, Fe, Gd, and Co make the products lose the RTP property (Fig. 1c) and lead to varying degrees of reduction or enhancement in photoactivated oxidase activity. The two CDs with HB and heme as the raw materials still exhibit the RTP property, among which the CDs prepared using heme exhibit the strongest photo-induced oxidase activity.

Meanwhile, the mechanism of the effect of different types of metal-doping reagents upon the catalytic performance of the prepared nanozymes has been examined. As phosphorescence exhibits a positive correlation with the photocatalytic activity of the prepared CDs, the presence of improper metal ions (Mn, Fe(Ⅱ)) in CDs structures reduced their fluorescence QY and lifetime and inhibited their oxidase activity. Zhang et al. synthesized a series of nitrogen-doped CDs and confirmed that their photo-oxidation activity was related to their phosphorescence QYs [33]. Meanwhile, research indicates that an efficient photo-driven oxidase-like nanozyme should possess a high yield and long lifetime of T1, and the yield and lifetime of T1 are directly related to the yield and lifetime of RTP. Therefore, a photo-driven oxidase-like nanozyme capable of stimulating the efficient generation of reactive oxygen species such as 1O2 may have a higher RTP yield and longer RTP lifetime [33]. Comparatively, the metal-ion containing small-molecule heme, is more beneficial to the enhancement of the phosphorescence and photo-induced enzyme activity of the products. Heme is known as natural metalloporphyrin and is the active center of the heme family of heme proteins (cytochromes, peroxisomes, myoglobin, and HB). The four pyrrole subunits of heme form a ring with a ferrous ion bound to the ring center and, it possesses rich active groups and a large conjugated structure. When used as a raw material, heme can easily interact with the silicon source for the polymerization carbonization reaction, which is beneficial to the formation of a carbon core with a conjugated rigid structure and the enhancement of the fluorescence and phosphorescence properties. Moreover, to ascertain whether the heme possesses photo-induced oxidase activity, thereby identifying the source of the enzymatic activity observed in the prepared CDs@heme. As illustrated in Fig. S1, heme exhibits photo-induced oxidase activity. However, under identical conditions, the photo-induced oxidase activity of heme is markedly inferior to that of CDs@heme. This indicates that CDs@heme has inherited the photo-induced enzyme activity of heme during the synthesis process and that doping with silicon can enhance this enzyme activity. The presence of heme residues on the surface of nanomaterials increases the exposure of active sites of the products, thus improving the catalytic activity. Compared with other proteins that are inactivated under high temperatures, heme maintains stability in a high-temperature environment in the process of carbonation and aggregation, moreover, its catalytic active sites and structure completeness are not affected by high temperature [34,35]. Therefore, the introduction of heme can effectively confer the photo-induced oxidase-like activity to CDs and its enzymatic activity is superior to that of heme.

To induce the phosphorescence of CDs@heme in the aqueous solution, TEOS and ammonia were introduced to further explore the preparation of aqueous-phase phosphorescent CDs@heme-TEOS. In this process, TEOS interact with water and CDs@heme, and ammonia catalyze the reaction. To be more specific, the entire process involves three steps, i.e., hydrolysis, condensation and polymerization reactions, among which the hydrolysis reaction plays the key role. In the initial stage, TEOS is hydrolyzed into silicon hydroxide, forming covalent (Si–O–C) and hydrogen bonds with CDs@heme-TEOS. The formation of these bonds embeds CDs@heme-TEOS into the silica network, enhancing the rigidity of the crosslinked framework, effectively suppressing nonradiative dissipation, and preventing the negative effects of dissolved oxygen, this leads to the aqueous-phase phosphorescence (Fig. S2) [36].

TEM was used to observe the morphology and structure of the prepared CDs@heme and CDs@heme-TEOS. CDs@heme and CDs@heme-TEOS were spherical and uniformly disperse with particle-size distribution in the range of 1.79–3.40 nm and 1.2–3 nm, respectively, and an average particle size of 2.50 and 2.46 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 a and b). [37]. We think the major reason for the smaller particle size of CDs@heme-TEOS compared to CDs@heme is goes to the “Shrinkage effect” during the modification process of TEOS. Shrestha Bhattacharya et al. claimed in their research that with the increase of TEOS concentration, the particle size of SiO2 particle reduces because of the change in nucleation and growth kinetics [38]. When TEOS forms silicon-oxygen network in the process of hydrolysis and condensation process of TEOS, and this process might lead to the structural rearrangement and densification of the material, resulting in the smaller particle size than that of the original CDs@heme. FT-IR and XPS spectra showed the surface structure, element composition and chemical composition. As shown in Fig. S3, both CDs@heme and CDs@heme-TEOS showed characterization absorption occurs at 1075.55, 1469.43 and 527.48 cm−1, which can be attributed to the presence of C–O/P–O/Si–O–Si/Si–O–C, heme and C–I [39–42].

XPS and FT-IR were used to further explore the chemical structure of the prepared CDs@heme and CDs@heme-TEOS. Characteristic peaks of CDs@heme are observed at 284.8, 531.51, 400.93, 102.01, 132.83, 726.26, and 618.24 eV, corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, Si 2p, P 2p, Fe 2p, and I 3d (Fig. S4), respectively, and their corresponding contents are 41.16 %, 33.16 %, 10.89 %, 5.84 %, 6.95 %, 0.32 %, and 1.69 %, respectively (Table S1). The C 1s bands can be deconvoluted into two peaks, corresponding to C–C (284.48 eV) and C–O (285.95 eV) (Fig. S5a). Meanwhile, the N 1s spectrum can be fitted into three peaks, at 398.88, 400.20, and 401.01 eV, corresponding to C–N–C, N–N, and C–N (Fig. S5b). The O 1s band is fitted into two peaks at 530.24 and 531.79 eV, attributed to the CO bond (Fig. S5c). The P 2p spectrum displays two characteristic peaks at 132.57 and 133.49 eV, which are ascribed to P–O and P–C/P–N groups, respectively (Fig. S5d) [43]. The Si 2p exhibits two characteristic peaks for C–Si–O and Si–O at 101.90 and 102.62 eV, respectively (Fig. S5e) [44]. The I 3d spectrum shows two characteristic peaks for I2 + and I− at 618.13 and 618.33 eV, respectively (Fig. S5f) [45,46].

Characteristic peaks of CDs@heme-TEOS are observed at 284.82, 532.82, 401.5, 103.4, 133.53, 709.07, and 618.87 eV, corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, Si 2p, P 2p, Fe 2p, and I 3d (Fig. S6), respectively, and their corresponding contents are 12.16 %, 58.33 %, 2.47 %, 23.02 %, 3.34 %, 0.47 %, and 0.22 %, respectively. The C 1s bands can be deconvoluted into two peaks, corresponding to C–O (284.78 eV) and C–OR (286.44 eV) (Fig. S7a) [47]. The N1s spectrum of the CDs@heme-TEOS membrane exhibits two major peaks at 399.83 eV and 401.72 eV, corresponding to the –N = , and CN (Fig. S7b) [48]. The O1s band of CDs@heme-TEOS (Fig. S7c) could be deconvoluted into two Gaussian peaks associated with CO and C–O–C, situated at 532.9 eV and 532.29 eV, respectively [49]. The P2p spectrum displays characteristic peaks at 133.59 eV, which are ascribed to P–C/P–N groups (Fig. S7d) [43]. The Si 2p exhibits characteristic peaks for Si–O at 103.42 eV (Fig. S7e) [44]. The I 3d spectrum shows characteristic peaks for I3 -1 at 618.84 eV (Fig. S7f) [50]. Owing to the Fe element is positioned at the center of the heme active site and its content is rather low. So, we used ICP-OES to quantify the specific amount of Fe in CDs@heme and CDs@heme-TEOS (Fig. S8). The experimental results showed that the concentration of Fe in every 2 mg/mL of CDs@heme and CDs@heme-TEOS was 11.268 mg/L and 12.482 mg/L, respectively, under the excitation wavelength of 259.939 nm, which verifies that Fe has been successfully incorporated into CDs@heme and CDs@heme-TEOS. Powder XRD is a rapid analytical technique used for the phase identification of any crystalline material that can provide the information on unit cell dimensions. The analysis revealed that was amorphous structure. Both the CDs@heme and CDs@heme-TEOS were observed diffraction plane (002) corresponding to 2θ = 22.5° depicted the presence of graphitic carbon, as shown in Fig. S9 [51]. Furthermore, a diffraction peak at 2θ = 31° of CDs@heme analogous to (211) plane of brookite phase (Fig. S9a) was also observed (JCPDS card no. 29-1360) [51].

UV–vis, fluorescence and phosphorescence spectroscopy have been used to examine the optical properties of CDs@heme and CDs@heme-TEOS. As shown in Fig. 2c, the absorption bands at 258–285 nm and 341 nm on UV–vis spectra of CDs@heme are attributed to the π→π∗ transition of the carbon core and n→π∗ transition of CO, respectively [52,53]. The presence of the CO/CN groups improve the self-selected orbit coupling, promoting the production of triplet excitons and ISC and the generation of RTP [53]. Under 365 nm UV-light irradiation, CDs@heme exhibits blue fluorescence in the solid state and blue fluorescence in the aqueous solution. After removing the UV-light source, the solid exhibits yellow-green phosphorescence, while the solution shows no obvious phosphorescence phenomenon.

The principle of fluorescence luminescence is that the arrival of electrons at the valence band caused by photo-excitation leads to the separation of electrons and holes, and when electrons and holes recombine, the fluorescence appears. As shown in Scheme 1, the electrons on the valence band of CDs@heme would transit to the conduction band when excited by light, and the electrons on the conduction band have two destinations: one is to the defect energy levels of CDs, and then back to the valence band, accompanied by blue fluorescence emission. The fluorescence wavelength peak is mainly at 420-450 nm.

Specifically, the interaction between the -C-N bond and the lone pair electrons on nitrogen facilitates n-π∗ transitions, promoting the formation of triplet excitons (T). When excited electrons transition from the ground state to the excited state, they undergo internal conversion back to the S1 excited state, followed by ISC to transfer from S to T, ultimately resulting in yellow-green phosphorescence [54,55]. TEM and XRD analyses indicate that the amorphous structure of CDs@heme may contain intertwined polymers that serve as a substrate to anchor the luminescent centers [56]. FTIR and XPS studies reveal that the surface of CDs@heme is rich in functional groups such as –OH, –NH2, CN, P–O, and –COOH, which provide effective binding sites for hydrogen bonding. These bonds form a hydrogen-bonding framework and stabilize the triplet-excited states further through intra-particle hydrogen bonding [57]. In addition, a high content of doped N and P elements has been confirmed, which enhances effective triplet exciton filling via ISC [58]. These features prevent triplet excitons from being quenched by atmospheric water and oxygen, thereby increasing the probability of phosphorescence emission.

Moreover, CDs@heme exhibits excitation-dependent fluorescence. The maximum emission wavelength appears at 446 nm under an optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm (Fig. 2d). For phosphorescence, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are 350 nm and 530 nm (Fig. S10a). The lifetime and QY of CDs@heme fluorescence are 6 ns and 7.59 %, respectively, and those of phosphorescence are 126 ms and 5.51 %, respectively(Fig. 2e and f). Based on CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates, the color coordinates of fluorescence and phosphorescence correspond to (0.178, 0.266) and (0.172, 0.209), respectively (Fig. S10b).

CDs@heme exhibits blue fluorescence with corresponding λEx/λEm at 370/446 nm. When CDs@heme interacts with carmine, the fluorescence emission of CDs@heme is quenched by carmine, and the quenching is directly proportional to the concentration of carmine. Based on this occurrence, a detection strategy for carmine was established in the range of 0–55.56 μM (Fig. 3 a–b), with regression equation as y = 197.85x + 1512 (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9919 and LOD = 0.33 μM, the detection limit is obtained by dividing the slope by three times the standard deviation (3 N/S)).

CDs@heme treated with TEOS exhibits yellow-green phosphorescence (λEx/λEm are 350/530 nm). When CDs@heme-TEOS interacts with carmine, the phosphorescence of CDs@heme-TEOS is proportionally quenched with increasing of carmine concentration in the range of 0–34.88 μM. Fig. 3c–d show the phosphorescence emission after the addition of carmine and corresponding calibration plots with regression equation as y = 123.79x + 830.01 (R2 = 0.9939 and LOD = 51 nM, the detection limit is obtained by dividing the slope by three times the standard deviation (3 N/S)). The mutual validation between fluorescence and phosphorescence signals of this dual-mode detection platform can effectively avoid or reduce the interference caused by non-target factors, highly enhancing the detection accuracy of complex samples.

Internal filtering effect (IFE) may be the mechanism for the quenching of CDs@heme fluorescence by carmine. As shown in Fig. S11, the UV–vis absorption spectra of carmine exhibit a broad absorption peak. When the absorption peak of the absorber (quencher carmine) in the detection system overlaps with the fluorescence emission wavelength (446 nm) and phosphorescence emission wavelength (530 nm), the IFE strategy turns the absorption signal into obvious fluorescence/phosphorescence signals and realizes sensitive detection [59].

To investigate the selectivity of the proposed detection system, the fluorescence/phosphorescence signals were examined in the presence of potential interfering substances, such as sugars (glucose, lactose), amino acids (glycine, lysine, etc.), metal ions (Pb2+, Ag+, etc.), and dyes (methyl red, neutral red, etc.). For this purpose, 30 μM of carmine and other interfering substances were mixed with CDs@heme and CDs@heme-TEOS. As shown in Fig. S12, metal ions and most other complex biomolecules actually do not affect the detection of carmine. CDs@heme and CDs@heme-TEOS show high responsiveness to carmine, proving the selectivity and effectiveness of this strategy to detect carmine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of oxidase activity of CDs@heme
ce:section.ce:para: With TMB as the chromogenic substrate, the enzymatic activity of CDs@heme was assessed. Under UV-light irradiation (365 nm), CDs@heme can catalyze the oxidation of colorless TMB into a blue product (oxTMB), affording a strong absorption peak at 652 nm. On the contrary, without UV-light irradiation, the mixture of TMB and CDs@heme remains colorless, proving the oxidase-like activity of CDs@heme (Fig. 4 a). At the same time, since the enzyme activity of the prepared CDs@heme is closely related to UV light, the UV light was switched on and off repeatedly during the enzyme activity triggering experiments. It can be seen that the photo-induced oxidase activity of CDs@heme is very stable when reacting with the enzyme substrate TMB, which can be effectively regulated by UV light (Fig. 4b).

The photo-induced oxidase activity of CDs@heme was further investigated through steady-state kinetic analysis. Typical Michaelis–Menten curves were obtained by plotting the corresponding initial reaction rates and substrate concentrations (Fig. S13a), and a Lineweaver–Burk plot was acquired by double inverting the data presented in Fig. S13a, as shown in Fig. S13b. The kinetic properties of the enzyme were evaluated theoretically through the Michaelis–Menten curves, accordingly, Vmax and Km were 3.4 × 10−9 M s−1 and 0.29 mM, respectively. Km is regarded as the indicator for the affinity between enzymes and substrates, thus, the lower the Km, the higher the affinity to substrates. Compared with some natural enzymes and artificial enzymes, CDs@heme possesses low Km and high Vmax, showing CDs@heme similar to oxidase mimics exhibits high affinity to TMB and high catalytic activity.

Regarding the catalytic mechanism of CDs@heme, the photo-induced oxidase–like activity might stem from the conversion of dissolved oxygen into ROS during the reaction. A series of experiments were carried out to verify this mechanism. First, compared the absorbance of an air-saturated solution, the absorbance of the solution blown with N2 for 1 min decreased, showing that the dissolved oxygen in the solution was involved in the photoactivated oxidation. The produced active ROS was removed through chemical scavengers. IPA, EDTA-2Na, Try, CuCl2, and AA were added to remove •OH, h+, 1O2, e− and O2 •-, respectively. The addition of EDTA-2Na, AA, and CuCl2 greatly weakened the oxidase-like activity of CDs@heme, demonstrating that the photo-oxidation was caused by h+, O2 •-, and e− (Fig. 4c). To probe the essence of the enzyme-like of CDs@heme, the powerful electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurements by utilizing 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as radical scavenger and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as the singlet oxygen (1O2) trapping substrate were performed, respectively. In the EPR test, the number and intensity of adduct peaks formed by different radicals captured by DMPO and TEMP may vary. A preliminary judgement can be made based on the number and intensity of peaks. For example, the EPR spectrum of the adduct formed by O2 •-captured by DMPO consists of four peaks, and the intensity ratio of the four peaks in the former is 1:2:2:1. The TEMP-captured 1O2 has three peaks with equal heights of 1:1:1 from the left to the right, and sometimes the last peak is slightly lower than the first two peaks due to the blockage of the solution [60]. Clear 1O2 and O2 •- signals can be observed (Fig. 4d), indicating the formation of 1O2 and O2 •- during the reaction. Therefore, the catalytic process can be inferred that the Fe–O/N active centers in CDs@heme trigger the decomposition of dissolved O2 and produces highly active 1O2 and O2 •-, which can rapidly oxidize TMB to a blue-colored oxidation state.

Experiments show that the interaction between TPN and CDs@heme would inhibit the photo-induced enzyme-like activity of CDs@heme, however, there was no significant effect on its fluorescence/phosphorescence, but the attenuation of its photo-induced oxidase activity resulting in a weakened or no color change of TMB. Therefore, a TPN colorimetric sensor based on the photo-induced oxidase activity of CDs@heme was established. To assess the feasibility of the sensor, with TPN as target, the signal response of catalytic system was plotted with the change in the TPN concentration, reflecting the response signal weakening or even disappearing with the increasing TPN concentration in the range of 0.015–25 μM (Fig. 5 a). The inset of Fig. 5a shows the linear calibration plot of the absorption at 652 nm, with a detection limit at 9.8 nM (The detection limit is obtained by dividing the slope by three times the standard deviation (3 N/S)) and linear equation as y = 0.0216x + 0.0714, corresponding to R2 = 0.9913.

The smartphone-assisted visual TPN sensor can better satisfy the need for on-site detection, thus, a smart hydrogel colorimetric sensing system was established, as shown in Fig. 5b. The PVM hydrogel was prepared using PVA, AM, MBA, and APS as the raw materials, and CDs@heme and TMB were thereafter embedded into the hydrogel. TPN of different concentrations was added into the hydrogel. With increasing TPN concentration, the color gradually faded (inset of Fig. 5b). Corresponding CIE plugin was used convert color signal of the hydrogel into L∗/a∗/b∗ signals after deducting the blanks. It was discovered that only the L∗ value exhibited a good linear relationship with the TPN concentration, with linear equation as y = −0.098x + 3.1022, corresponding to R2 = 0.9969.

The selective responsiveness is critical to sensors. The common interfering substances such as metal ions, amino acids, and sugars, widely exist in the biological system. As shown in Fig. S14, metal ions and most biochemical substances do not affect the colorimetric detection of TPN. This might be because the inhibitory effect of TPN on the CDs@heme–TMB colorimetric system involves two aspects. First, as a sulfhydryl compound, TPN exhibits strong combination with the metal ions doped on the surface of CDs@heme, inhibiting the catalyzing active sites of CDs@heme, reducing the formation of oxTMB and finally leading to the weakened or no color change of TMB. Second, the reducing property of TPN converts oxTMB into a colorless compound.

The feasibility of the strategy was further proven using actual samples. TPN enteric-coated tablets were used to perform spiking recovery tests. As shown in Table 1 , the recovery rate of TPN enteric-coated tablets (0.1 g/tablet) was determined as 99.85 %–109.21 %, with RSD% ≤ 2.0, illustrating the satisfactory applicability of this CDs@heme-based colorimetric detection strategy to TPN in biological samples.

CDs@heme was further used as a high-quality counterfeiting ink for advanced encryption. Based on the non-substrate-assisted RTP property and photo-induced enzyme-like activity, a multi-layered information anticounterfeiting/encryption system was established. The same amount of CDs@heme and conventional CDs (with blue fluorescence only) were dissolved into water to prepare Ink 1 and Ink 2, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 , the area of number “666” was covered by Ink 1, and the remaining area was covered by Ink 2. Under UV-light irradiation (365 nm), the card exhibited blue fluorescence with number “888”. When the UV light was turned off, the area covered by Ink 1 emitted yellow–green phosphorescence and showed the code number “666”. The color developer prepared by TMB (pH = 3) was applied to the area covered by Ink 1 and Ink 2. After 1 min of UV-light irradiation, the blue code “666” could be seen by naked eye because of the photocatalytic activity of CDs@heme. These observation results prove the application potential of CDs@heme in information anticounterfeiting and encryption. Compared with traditional anticounterfeiting techniques, the multilayered (fluorescence, RTP, and colorimetric) information encryption can realize higher-level information security. Furthermore, in order to ascertain the stability and reproducibility of fluorescence and phosphorescence in this anti-counterfeiting system, an examination was conducted on test paper. As illustrated in Fig. S15, the fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs@heme on the test paper remained discernible following numerous repeated irradiations with UV lamps over a period of five days.

In this paper, we report the rapid synthesis of CDs@heme with non-substrate-assisted phosphorescence and photo-induced oxidase–like activity through a microwave method. Through a series of characterization methods, it was discovered that CDs@heme solution emitted blue fluorescence under UV-light irradiation (365 nm) and its solid powder emitted yellow–green phosphorescence without UV light, and the phosphorescence emission time reached 13 s, with fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of CDs at 6 ns and 126 ms. Furthermore, water-soluble phosphorescent CDs@heme-TEOS was prepared by compounding the prepared CDs@heme with TEOS. Based on IFE, carmine could effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs@heme and phosphorescence of CDs@heme-TEOS. This multiple-parameter or multi-responsive detection mode can effectively enhance the selectivity and accuracy of the detection with detection limits of fluorescence and phosphorescence at 330 and 51 nM. Along with the inhibitory effect of TPN to the photo-induced oxidase-like activity of CDs@heme, a rapid and simple colorimetric platform to TPN was established in the concentration range of 0.015–25 μM, with a detection limit of 9.8 nM. Furthermore, based on these results, a fluorescence/phosphorescence/colorimetric three-layered information encryption system has been proposed, which provides a powerful tool for high security-level counterfeiting applications. This study offers basis for the synthesis of novel non-substrate phosphorescent nanozyme and expands its applications in biosensing and information counterfeiting fields.

Changpeng Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yan Jiao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Dan Zhao: Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xincai Xiao: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
This work was funded by the Fund for Academic Innovation Teams of South-Central Minzu University (XTZ24027) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21978329).
